summary: I was born in Myanmar in 1999, in a jungle surrounded by mountains. It was not a safe place because of war, persecution, and a government dictator. My family had to flee from our village to escape the dangers there, especially from the Burmese soldiers. Myanmar is where my journey began, but it was also the place that forced us to leave for safety.

The Burmese soldiers were a constant threat to my family when we lived in Myanmar. They surrounded our village, forcing us to run and hide multiple times. I remember the day they found our hiding place and burned it down; my parents had to carry me on their backs to escape. Their presence made life unsafe and pushed us to seek refuge elsewhere.

In 2003, my family moved to the Karenni refugee camp called Mae Surin in Thailand because of the civil war in Burma. We built a house with bamboo and leaves, and the camp provided shelter and education for people like us. Although life there was better than before, we still faced challenges like limited freedom and food shortages. We stayed in that camp for eight years before moving to the United States.

Thailand was where my family found refuge after fleeing Myanmar in 2003. We lived in the Karenni refugee camp there for eight years, where I had the chance to attend school despite the hardships. Thailand gave us a temporary home, but we still faced restrictions like not being allowed to leave the camp or work. It was a place of transition before we immigrated to the United States in 2012.

In 2012, my family decided to move to the United States because we heard it offered better opportunities and education. After multiple interviews and tests, we left the refugee camp and traveled by plane through Korea and New York before arriving in Cleveland, Ohio. The U.S. became our new home, where I attended school and eventually moved to Minnesota for a better community. I am grateful to my family for making this journey for a better life.

Our journey to the United States included a stop in Korea, where we took a connecting flight from Thailand to New York. Although we only stayed briefly, Korea was an important part of our long travel to reach America. It was one of the three flights we took over two days to finally arrive in the U.S.

New York City was another stop on our journey to the United States. After flying from Korea, we landed in New York before taking a final flight to Cleveland, Ohio. Although we didn’t stay long, New York was part of the two-day trip that brought us to our new home.

We arrived in Cleveland, Ohio on March 22, 2012, and I remember it as a wonderful city with a beautiful downtown. I started school there at Thomas Jefferson High School in 8th grade, but it was very challenging because I didn’t know English. After two years, my family moved to Lakewood due to high housing costs and safety concerns in Cleveland. Cleveland was our first home in America and a place where I began adapting to a new life.

When I first went to school in the U.S., I attended Thomas Jefferson High School in Cleveland starting in 8th grade in 2013. It was difficult because I didn’t speak English and struggled to understand others. The students were mostly Spanish and Black, and while some were mean, my teachers were kind and encouraging. I could only attend for two years before moving to another school.

After living in Cleveland for two years, my family moved to Lakewood, Ohio because housing was too expensive and there was a lot of violence where we lived. In Lakewood, I attended Lakewood High School as a 10th grader. I was often the only Asian student in my classes, which made school difficult and sometimes lonely. Despite these challenges, it was a safer and more affordable place for my family.

In November 2014, my family moved to Saint Paul, Minnesota because we heard there were many Karen people there who could help us. Moving to Minnesota gave us a stronger community and support network. I started attending Washington Technology Magnet School there and felt more connected to my culture and people. Minnesota became a place where I could continue my education and feel more at home.

After moving to Saint Paul, Minnesota in 2014, I attended Washington Technology Magnet School. This school became an important part of my education and helped me continue to improve. I am thankful for the opportunities it gave me to learn and grow in a new environment. It represents a positive chapter in my journey after so many moves.

The Burmese civil war was the main reason my family had to flee Myanmar and seek refuge in Thailand in 2003. The conflict made it impossible for us to live safely in our village because of the violence and persecution. It forced us to live in a refugee camp for eight years before we could immigrate to the United States. The civil war shaped much of my early life and the hardships my family faced.
